SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The technical approach (shared/private LSTMs plus an adversarial task discriminator) is plausible and the paper gives a clear optimization objective (Eq. 13 and final loss Eq. 15) and training recipe (use of gradient reversal layer). Experiments on 16 datasets with consistent embeddings (200d GloVe) and train/dev/test splits support the main empirical claims (Section 5.4). However, some methodological details are omitted (no training epochs, random seeds, or statistical significance tests) and equations/text contain formatting/repetition issues that obscure small derivations, so minor issues remain but they do not undermine the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The work combines a shared–private multi-task architecture with adversarial training and explicitly extends adversarial loss to the multi-class (multi-task) setting (Section 4.2, Eq. 13), which is a non-trivial adaptation of prior binary adversarial/domain-adaptation methods. Prior references to adversarial domain adaptation and shared-private MTL are acknowledged (Related Work), so the contribution is a clear extension rather than a wholly novel paradigm.

CLARITY.justification: High-level structure is understandable (LSTM model, FS-MTL and SP-MTL, adversarial extension, experiments) and main components are described with equations (e.g., Eqs. 1–4, 8–10, 12–15). However the provided text contains duplicated/repeated passages, formatting glitches, and references to figures/tables that are not visible here, which reduces readability and makes some parts harder to follow.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experimental scope is strong in breadth (16 datasets) and hyperparameter search is described (Section 5.3), and the paper reports average improvements (Section 5.4). Nevertheless, the manuscript lacks important depth such as ablation studies isolating the effect of LDiff vs. LAdv, sensitivity analyses, training details (epochs, convergence), statistical significance, and discussion of limitations or failure cases, limiting reproducibility and interpretability.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—learning task-invariant shared features in multi-task NLP—and shows consistent empirical gains across many datasets (Section 5). While useful and likely to be adopted by practitioners working on MTL, the contribution is an evolutionary improvement rather than a transformative change to the field.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (adversarial multi-task learning for text classification) and methods (LSTMs, adversarial loss, gradient reversal) are squarely within the scope and expectations of a top-tier NLP/ML venue. Datasets, baselines, and evaluation setup described fit the standard community practices (Sections 5.1 and 5.4).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its approach against several baselines including single-task LSTM variants and multi-task methods (e.g., MT-CNN, MT-DNN, FS-MTL, SP-MTL) and reports per-task and average error rates (Section 5.4). Comparisons appear to use the same embeddings and data splits, but the manuscript omits statistical tests and some implementation/training details that would fully guarantee fairness and make it harder to assess competitive performance against all recent alternatives.
